feed.title: Jani’s Magik
feed.subtitle: My blog about Django and such

entry.title: Django login with OTP
entry.content:
This is simple example how to build email based OTP (one time password) into Django authentication.

Solution isn’t perfect but it is relatively simple and leverages as much as possible standard Django authentication mechanism.

First create a project and app. Add app to the settings.py INSTALLED_APPS setting. Also run migrations and create superuser.

Open up your app views.py and create index view with login required decorator since that’s what we use to verify that login works as expected:

And add just created view to project’s urls.py. In this example we’re not writing any urls to app:

Start the development server and try to access your site. Django should try to access login url but can’t find since it’s not yet specified.

Add login views to your project’s urls.py:

If you now try to access your index page you should get error about missing template. That’s expected since Django doesn’t provide templates for authentication.

Let’s create template and add field for OTP input at the same time. Even OTP isn’t used at this point we can add it. Django will just ignore it while logging user in.

Create new file in your app templates/registration/login.html location with contents:

Make a note for few differences from most common version of login screen. Password field is created more manually than usually. That is because we need to pass password back to login form while waiting for OTP. Of course this also causes side-effect that if password was wrong field is not cleared.

After adding this you should be able to login to your site just fine. Enter url http://localhost:8000/accounts/logout/ to logout.

Now the “hard part”. How to make OTP? We could define our custom login view that would make OTP and send it as an email. This solution uses different approach and uses partially custom authentication form that will take OTP into account.

So create forms.py in your app:

The trick here is to inherit standard AuthenticationForm and add OTP input field to it. In form clean method we first check does session contain OTP value. If not create new one and store it in the user session. In this example OTP is hardcoded as “1234”.

You need to implement your own version that creates secure passwords to you. And send it to user email.

As a last step we claim that form is not valid (since OTP is missing) by raising validation error.

Now you will be represented same login form as previously, prefilled with username and password and waiting only for OTP.

But now final piece of this puzzle is missing. You need to override default login view.

So edit your project’s urls.py to contain override:

And as a final step, remember to make settings to match your smtp provider: so add smtp settings to settings.py:

And that’s it. Now you should have OTP login system in effect.

I gathered here some important information to note what this OTP addition does and what it doesn’t do.

Username and password are sent back and forth few times and it is always in plain text. Though it will be plain text even on standard submit so that itself isn’t security issue. Hopefully you protect your users usernames and passwords by using HTTPS protocol.

There is small issue how OTP is handled. OTP is tied to current session so you can login as a different user with OTP sent to you, just change username and password since authenticated user is not saved into session. So when OTP is checked it relies that username and password are the same as on previous attempt where OTP was generated. This would be relatively easy to implement to store user id into session to verify that session OTP matches user that requested it.

This example also doesn’t expire OTP which you should do. After all they’re meant to be short lived.
entry.summary: This is simple example how to build email based OTP (one time password) into Django authentication.

entry.title: Developing Django apps with docker-compose and pipenv

link.title: Developing Django apps with docker-compose and pipenv

entry.content:
These are instruction how to setup and start developing your applications with Django, Docker and docker-compose using PostgreSQL as database backend.

Start with creating your project directory and empty Pipfile:

If you’re on Windows you don’t have touch but can uew :

Next you need to create Dockefile for your app itself. Note that you can pick any version of Python that you can find from Docker hub and put it to FROM line. Full range of Python images can be found at https://hub.docker.com/_/python.

Next you need to create docker-compose.yml. For Postgres you use plain image and you can choose any version which you like. You can fine full range of images at https://hub.docker.com/_/postgres.

At this point you do have minimal setup for Python and Postgres, now it is time to build the whole package for the first time to bootstrap your environment.

Next you need to install first packages in container, in this case Django and psycopg2-binary libraries. You can use same command to install additional packages as well in the future.

After installation finishes you will see changes in Pipfile and Pipfile.lock. If you don’t something went wrong. Now it’s important to note that image we do have doesn’t contain these new packages. They were installed in container that was removed after pipenv install finished it’s job.

So rebuild the image. Remember to do this every time you add new packages to your pipfile.

After build finishes successfully it’s time to setup Django project and first app:

Now you have to edit myproject/settings.py and it’s DATABASES setting to use PostgreSQL instead of sqlite:

There are are few things to note in these settings. HOST is actually service name from docker-compose.yml part. Adding dependency to db service docker-compose will inject internal ip of the service within your web container hosts file.

And now you can start developing your app, add myapp to INSTALLED_APPS in your settings.py and start coding.

Running development server.

And now you can point your browser to http://localhost:8000/

Following list has few additional and useful commands to start working with Django.

Making migrations.

Migrating database.

Creating superuser.

Upgrading package.
entry.summary: These are instruction how to setup and start developing your applications with Django, Docker and docker-compose using PostgreSQL as database backend.

entry.title: Union models naïve way
entry.content:
How to handle union of two (or more), totally different models?

This is naïve approach but it is relatively fast and works well for simple cases without adding too much complexity for actual code.

Let’s define two models in models.py:

We want to have models to be sorted alphabetically by name and title (mixing them) and return real instances of both models as a final result.

The queries would look like this:

items contains now items we wanted and in order as we wanted. A good thing is that items can be paginated or sliced. But there is a problem, how we can get back our model instances?

Let’s enhance our queries a bit with additional annotation:

Now items are annotated with source name of the object. So now we know which object is in the question. But how we get our instances back, since items is still a list of values? We could loop over items and query objects one by one but that might generate quite a lot of queries even for a short list.

We do an assumption that all items are used so we pull them in as a list instead of queryset.

Now the trick is to use __in lookup expression to make minimal amount of queries:

At this point we do have our lists of real objects. Note that they are really listt and not querysets because we want to loop over those lists later on.

And the naïve piece of code to combine list of objects in correct order with brute-force method:

After that loop finishes objs will contain all objects in orderly fashion and can be used for example in template to list them.

Most probably you should refactor object retrieval if you do have more than few models to union or if you use it in more than one place.
entry.summary: How to handle union of two (or more), totally different models?

entry.title: Django forms and HTML
entry.content:
Sometimes people are asking how to make specific field widget and how to add specific styles to widgets. Many try to use form instance and modify widget attributes there. And it does work. But it also does put pieces of representation in your application code which makes harder for example user interface designers to actually make user interfaces for your beautiful app.

Forms in Django are meant to be data validation and coercion tool in the first place. And they’re really good at it. An ability to render forms as HTML is a nice bonus but nothing forces you to use taht feature if you want to do something more than simple widget rendering.

But how Django knows how to get data from POST request then if widgets are not renderd by Django itself?

Let’s define very simple Django form first:

Now corresponding HTML form would look like:

The key here is HTML form input field name attributes.

That’s how Django knows that which values to map from HTTP POST data to which field. This gives you a lot of opportunities to tweak your form and it’s representation. Best of all is that you can give just name of the fields to web designers and ask them to make cool forms for you.
entry.summary: Sometimes people are asking how to make specific field widget and how to add specific styles to widgets. Many try to use form instance and modify widget attributes there. And it does work. But it also does put pieces of representation in your application code which makes harder for example user interface designers to actually make user interfaces for your beautiful app.

entry.title: Django date selector field
entry.content:
I was asked how to make a field in Django that accepts one of three dates, 15, 30 and 45 days from today.

Solution turned out to be rather simple after all.

First define the model in models.py:

If you need a form for such model it’s rather easy to do.

Create following form in your forms.py:

But I want to do this in admin… Not a problem, Django has that covered for you.

And now if you open up admin you see your date field with only three options to select. 15 days, 30 days or 45 days counted from today.
entry.summary: I was asked how to make a field in Django that accepts one of three dates, 15, 30 and 45 days from today.
